payload.audience: application developers using dali-ui public APIs

content_quality.default_object_model: Use Dali::Ui::View rather than Dali::Actor for app-facing object examples.
content_quality.guide_assumption: The reader is using dali-ui to build applications.

example_candidates.lookup_instruction: Open this file only if more concrete usage evidence is needed.
Open this file only if more concrete usage evidence is needed.
Open this file only if more concrete usage evidence is needed.

code_verification: receiver method exists on the receiver type
static methods are called statically and member methods are called on valid instances
assignment target is compatible with the method return type
argument count, argument type, and overload shape match the public API
enum owner and enum value both exist
property constants use the correct owner and value type
signal callback signatures match the public signal typedef
code blocks do not rely on inferred namespaces or overloads from naming alone
draft_requirements: Write enough practical detail for application developers to use the feature in a dali-ui app.
Ground natural-language behavior claims in the actual public headers, implementation files, samples, or UTCs when the context pack is not enough.
Before writing examples, inspect the referenced public headers and relevant sample files when needed.
Prefer examples copied or adapted from existing samples when they match the feature; otherwise keep examples small and verify every API use.
After drafting, review the completed Markdown line by line against repository evidence and fix inaccurate explanations or code before returning.
repair_requirements: Repair only the problematic spans and the smallest surrounding context needed; do not rewrite the whole document.
For each listed issue, use the repository evidence and context pack to verify the corrected wording or code before returning.
If an issue reveals the same API misuse nearby, fix the nearby equivalent misuse too; do not make unrelated style rewrites.
After repair, re-check the edited lines against public headers, implementation files, samples, or UTCs when needed.

rules: Use dali-ui symbols as the app-facing API surface.
Use dali-core and dali-adaptor symbols only as setup/background unless the usage pattern explicitly marks them preferred.
Prefer View-based examples over raw Actor object-model examples.

usage_patterns.intent: Create and configure the primary object using public dali-ui APIs.
usage_patterns.usage_note: Prefer typed constructors and setters when available.

usage_patterns.intent: React to user or resource events through public signals.
usage_patterns.usage_note: Use signal connection APIs only with their owning trait or class.

rendered_prompt:
You are reviewing an existing DALi dali-ui application developer guide draft.
This is Phase 2.3.5: source-grounded prose review.

Primary goal:
- Improve natural-language accuracy and guide flow using repository evidence.
- Preserve the document's useful structure and examples whenever possible.
- Do not perform a full rewrite.
- Keep the revised user-facing guide in en only.
- Remove Korean, Japanese, Chinese, or other non-English prose from the revised guide.

Feature target:
- Feature: Signals (signals)
- Category: utilities
- Guide profile: dali-ui-app
- Audience: application developers using dali-ui public APIs

Review rules:
- Review every natural-language paragraph line by line against public headers, implementation files, samples, and UTCs when needed.
- Accuracy is the first priority. Flow is the second priority.
- Edit the smallest unit needed: word, sentence, paragraph, or section-local addition/removal.
- If a word-level change is enough, change only the word.
- If a sentence-level change is enough, change only the sentence.
- If an entire paragraph is based on a wrong premise, revise, add, or remove that paragraph.
- Preserve code blocks when possible.
- If prose can be corrected to match an already accurate code block, prefer changing prose.
- If the code block itself makes the prose inaccurate or breaks the guide flow, rewrite only the smallest necessary part of that code block and verify it against public headers/source.
- Do not invent APIs.
- Do not add generic filler just to make the document longer.
- Do not change section structure unless prose_review.allow_section_restructure is true or the current prose is clearly misleading.
- Prefer localized edits when prose_review.localized_edits_only is true.
- Preserve code blocks especially when prose_review.preserve_code_blocks is true.
- Prefer Dali::Ui::View, typed setters, fluent chaining, and view-tree idioms for app-facing guidance.
- Treat dali-core and dali-adaptor APIs as reference/context unless the feature explicitly requires them.

Context summary:
{
  "content_quality": {
    "overview_style": "very_short",
    "require_table_of_contents": true,
    "require_rich_api_examples": true,
    "require_backticks_for_api_names": true,
    "topic_sections_must_be_feature_specific": true,
    "avoid_mechanical_api_listing": true,
    "default_object_model": "Use Dali::Ui::View rather than Dali::Actor for app-facing object examples.",
    "guide_assumption": "The reader is using dali-ui to build applications."
  },
  "prose_review": {
    "enabled": true,
    "apply_to": "all",
    "preserve_code_blocks": true,
    "localized_edits_only": true,
    "allow_section_restructure": false,
    "output_review_report": true,
    "fail_open_on_parse_error": true
  },
  "surface_usage": {
    "primary_surface": "dali-ui",
    "rules": [
      "Use dali-ui symbols as the app-facing API surface.",
      "Use dali-core and dali-adaptor symbols only as setup/background unless the usage pattern explicitly marks them preferred.",
      "Prefer View-based examples over raw Actor object-model examples."
    ],
    "symbol_counts": {
      "dali-core": 180
    }
  },
  "usage_patterns": [
    {
      "avoid_symbols": [],
      "confidence": 0.85,
      "id": "signals_basic_setup",
      "intent": "Create and configure the primary object using public dali-ui APIs.",
      "preferred_symbols": [
        "Dali::FunctorDelegate::New",
        "Dali::RenderCallback::New"
      ],
      "property_values": [],
      "source": "feature_owned_public_api",
      "usage_note": "Prefer typed constructors and setters when available."
    },
    {
      "avoid_symbols": [],
      "confidence": 0.8,
      "id": "signals_signals",
      "intent": "React to user or resource events through public signals.",
      "preferred_symbols": [
        "Dali::ConnectionTracker",
        "Dali::ConnectionTracker::ConnectionTracker",
        "Dali::ConnectionTracker::DisconnectAll",
        "Dali::ConnectionTracker::GetConnectionCount",
        "Dali::ConnectionTracker::Impl",
        "Dali::ConnectionTracker::Impl::DisconnectAllAndReset",
        "Dali::ConnectionTracker::Impl::Erase",
        "Dali::ConnectionTracker::Impl::GetSize"
      ],
      "property_values": [],
      "source": "feature_owned_public_api",
      "usage_note": "Use signal connection APIs only with their owning trait or class."
    }
  ],

Original Markdown draft:
---
title: Signals
sidebar_label: Signals
category: utilities
---

# Signals

Signals let dali-ui application code react to view, input, resource, and lifecycle events while keeping connection lifetime explicit and safe.

## Table of Contents

- [Connect View Events](#connect-view-events)
- [Track Connection Lifetime](#track-connection-lifetime)
- [Use Lambdas, Member Functions, and Free Functions](#use-lambdas-member-functions-and-free-functions)
- [Inspect and Disconnect Connections](#inspect-and-disconnect-connections)
- [Expose Small Application Signals](#expose-small-application-signals)
- [Use Return Values Carefully](#use-return-values-carefully)

## Connect View Events

In a dali-ui application, the usual entry point is a `Dali::Ui::View` or a class derived from it. View-facing APIs expose typed `Dali::Signal` objects. Application code connects a callback with the same argument and return shape as the signal.

A typical controller inherits from `Dali::ConnectionTracker` and connects view signals during setup:

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/dali-ui-foundation.h>

using namespace Dali;
using namespace Dali::Ui;

class PageController : public ConnectionTracker
{
public:
  void Setup(Label title)
  {
    title.FocusChangedSignal().Connect(this, &PageController::OnFocusChanged);
  }

private:
  void OnFocusChanged(View view, bool focused)
  {
    view.SetBackgroundColor(focused ? UiColor(0x3366CC) : UiColor(0x222222));
  }
};
```

For input-style signals that return `bool`, return `true` when the event has been handled and should be consumed, or `false` when other handlers may continue processing it:

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/dali-ui-foundation.h>

using namespace Dali;
using namespace Dali::Ui;

class KeyController : public ConnectionTracker
{
public:
  void Setup(Label label)
  {
    label.SetFocusable(true);

    label.KeyEventSignal().Connect(this, [this](View view, KeyEvent event) -> bool
    {
      return OnKeyEvent(view, event);
    });
  }

private:
  bool OnKeyEvent(View view, KeyEvent event)
  {
    if(event.GetState() == KeyEvent::DOWN)
    {
      view.SetBackgroundColor(UiColor(0x5588EE));
      return true;
    }

    return false;
  }
};
```

`Dali::Signal<void(Arg0)>`, `Dali::Signal<void(Arg0, Arg1)>`, and `Dali::Signal<Ret(Arg0, Arg1)>` all require the callback signature to match the signal signature. For example, a focus signal callback receives `View, bool`, while a key signal callback receives `View, KeyEvent` and returns `bool`.

## Track Connection Lifetime

`Dali::ConnectionTracker` is the primary lifetime tool for application code. When a callback is connected with `this` and the receiving object derives from `Dali::ConnectionTracker`, the connection is associated with that receiver. Destroying the tracker disconnects the slots it owns.

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/dali-ui-foundation.h>

using namespace Dali;
using namespace Dali::Ui;

class ImageStatus : public ConnectionTracker
{
public:
  void Bind(ImageView image, Label status)
  {
    mStatus = status;

    image.ResourceReadySignal().Connect(this, [this](View)
    {
      ++mReadyCount;
      mStatus.SetText("Ready count: " + String(std::to_string(mReadyCount).c_str()));
    });
  }

private:
  Label    mStatus;
  uint32_t mReadyCount{0};
};
```

A `Dali::ConnectionTracker` can also be passed explicitly when connecting a function object to a `Dali::Signal`. This is useful when the receiver object does not need a separate member function:

```cpp
#include <dali/public-api/signals/dali-signal.h>
#include <dali/public-api/signals/connection-tracker.h>

using namespace Dali;

class Loader : public ConnectionTracker
{
public:
  using ReadySignalType = Signal<void(uint32_t)>;

  ReadySignalType& ReadySignal()
  {
    return mReadySignal;
  }

  void ConnectHandlers()
  {
    mReadySignal.Connect(this, [this](uint32_t itemCount)
    {
      mLastItemCount = itemCount;
    });
  }

  void Complete(uint32_t itemCount)
  {
    mReadySignal.Emit(itemCount);
  }

private:
  ReadySignalType mReadySignal;
  uint32_t        mLastItemCount{0};
};
```

`Dali::ConnectionTracker::DisconnectAll()` disconnects every signal currently tracked by the receiver. Use it when a controller leaves a page or rebinds to a new group of views before destruction.

## Use Lambdas, Member Functions, and Free Functions

A `Dali::Signal` supports several callback forms. In application code, member functions and lambdas with a `Dali::ConnectionTracker` are the most useful because their lifetime is tied to the receiver.

Member function callback:

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/dali-ui-foundation.h>

using namespace Dali;
using namespace Dali::Ui;

class FocusRing : public ConnectionTracker
{
public:
  void Attach(View target)
  {
    target.FocusChangedSignal().Connect(this, &FocusRing::OnFocusChanged);
  }

private:
  void OnFocusChanged(View target, bool focused)
  {
    target.SetOpacity(focused ? 1.0f : 0.6f);
  }
};
```

Lambda callback:

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/dali-ui-foundation.h>

using namespace Dali;
using namespace Dali::Ui;

class SelectionController : public ConnectionTracker
{
public:
  void Attach(InteractiveTrait& trait)
  {
    trait.ClickedSignal().Connect(this, [this](View selectedView, InputEvent) -> bool
    {
      mSelected = selectedView;
      return true;
    });
  }

private:
  View mSelected;
};
```

Free function callback:

```cpp
#include <dali/public-api/signals/dali-signal.h>

using namespace Dali;

namespace
{
void OnRefresh()
{
}
}

void ConnectRefresh(Signal<void()>& refreshSignal)
{
  refreshSignal.Connect(&OnRefresh);
}
```

For function objects that need type-erased storage, `Dali::FunctorDelegate::New()` creates a delegate owned by the signal connection:

```cpp
#include <dali/public-api/signals/dali-signal.h>
#include <dali/public-api/signals/connection-tracker.h>
#include <dali/public-api/signals/functor-delegate.h>

using namespace Dali;

class RefreshController : public ConnectionTracker
{
public:
  void Bind(Signal<void()>& refreshSignal)
  {
    refreshSignal.Connect(this, FunctorDelegate::New([this]
    {
      ++mRefreshCount;
    }));
  }

private:
  uint32_t mRefreshCount{0};
};
```

## Inspect and Disconnect Connections

`Dali::Signal` inherits connection inspection from `Dali::SignalMixin`. Use `Empty()` when you only need to know whether any slots are connected, and `GetConnectionCount()` when diagnostic code needs the exact count.

```cpp
#include <dali/public-api/signals/dali-signal.h>

using namespace Dali;

class RefreshModel
{
public:
  using ChangedSignalType = Signal<void()>;

  ChangedSignalType& ChangedSignal()
  {
    return mChangedSignal;
  }

  bool HasListeners() const
  {
    return !mChangedSignal.Empty();
  }

  uint32_t ListenerCount() const
  {
    return mChangedSignal.GetConnectionCount();
  }

  void NotifyChanged()
  {
    mChangedSignal.Emit();
  }

private:
  ChangedSignalType mChangedSignal;
};
```

Disconnect a specific function or member function by passing the same receiver and callback shape used for `Connect()`:

```cpp
#include <dali/public-api/signals/dali-signal.h>
#include <dali/public-api/signals/connection-tracker.h>

using namespace Dali;

class RefreshHandler : public ConnectionTracker
{
public:
  void Attach(Signal<void()>& signal)
  {
    signal.Connect(this, &RefreshHandler::OnRefresh);
  }

  void Detach(Signal<void()>& signal)
  {
    signal.Disconnect(this, &RefreshHandler::OnRefresh);
  }

private:
  void OnRefresh()
  {
  }
};
```

When the receiver owns several connections, `Dali::ConnectionTracker::GetConnectionCount()` gives the number currently tracked by that receiver, and `Dali::ConnectionTracker::DisconnectAll()` clears them together:

```cpp
#include <dali/public-api/signals/dali-signal.h>
#include <dali/public-api/signals/connection-tracker.h>

using namespace Dali;

class PageBindings : public ConnectionTracker
{
public:
  void Bind(Signal<void()>& opened, Signal<void()>& closed)
  {
    opened.Connect(this, &PageBindings::OnOpened);
    closed.Connect(this, &PageBindings::OnClosed);
  }

  void Reset()
  {
    if(GetConnectionCount() > 0u)
    {
      DisconnectAll();
    }
  }

private:
  void OnOpened()
  {
  }

  void OnClosed()
  {
  }
};
```

## Expose Small Application Signals

Application-level components can expose their own typed `Dali::Signal` members. Define a type alias, return the signal by reference, and emit it from the component when the state changes.

```cpp
#include <dali/public-api/signals/dali-signal.h>
#include <dali/public-api/signals/connection-tracker.h>

using namespace Dali;

class SelectionModel
{
public:
  using SelectionChangedSignalType = Signal<void(uint32_t)>;

  SelectionChangedSignalType& SelectionChangedSignal()
  {
    return mSelectionChangedSignal;
  }

  void Select(uint32_t index)
  {
    if(mSelectedIndex != index)
    {
      mSelectedIndex = index;
      mSelectionChangedSignal.Emit(mSelectedIndex);
    }
  }

private:
  SelectionChangedSignalType mSelectionChangedSignal;
  uint32_t                   mSelectedIndex{0u};
};

class SelectionViewController : public ConnectionTracker
{
public:
  void Bind(SelectionModel& model)
  {
    model.SelectionChangedSignal().Connect(this, &SelectionViewController::OnSelectionChanged);
  }

private:
  void OnSelectionChanged(uint32_t index)
  {
    mLastSelection = index;
  }

  uint32_t mLastSelection{0u};
};
```

Use the exact `Dali::Signal` specialization that matches the event contract:

```cpp
using StartedSignalType  = Dali::Signal<void()>;
using ProgressSignalType = Dali::Signal<void(float)>;
using AcceptedSignalType = Dali::Signal<bool(uint32_t, uint32_t)>;
```

`Dali::Signal<void()>` emits notifications without data. `Dali::Signal<void(Arg0)>` and `Dali::Signal<void(Arg0, Arg1)>` pass data to listeners. `Dali::Signal<Ret(Arg0, Arg1)>` collects a return value from connected callbacks.

## Use Return Values Carefully

A returning `Dali::Signal` returns the value from the last callback that runs. If no callbacks are connected, it returns a default-constructed value for the return type.

```cpp
#include <dali/public-api/signals/dali-signal.h>
#include <dali/public-api/signals/connection-tracker.h>

using namespace Dali;

class PermissionModel
{
public:
  using AllowSignalType = Signal<bool(uint32_t)>;

  AllowSignalType& AllowSignal()
  {
    return mAllowSignal;
  }

  bool CanOpen(uint32_t itemId)
  {
    return mAllowSignal.Emit(itemId);
  }

private:
  AllowSignalType mAllowSignal;
};

class PermissionController : public ConnectionTracker
{
public:
  void Bind(PermissionModel& model)
  {
    model.AllowSignal().Connect(this, &PermissionController::OnAllow);
  }

private:
  bool OnAllow(uint32_t itemId)
  {
    return itemId != 0u;
  }
};
```

For two-argument returning signals, `EmitOr()` is available and combines callback results with logical OR behavior. This is useful for consumed-style application events where any handler may accept the event:

```cpp
#include <dali/public-api/signals/dali-signal.h>
#include <dali/public-api/signals/connection-tracker.h>

using namespace Dali;

class CommandRouter
{
public:
  using CommandSignalType = Signal<bool(uint32_t, uint32_t)>;

  CommandSignalType& CommandSignal()
  {
    return mCommandSignal;
  }

  bool Dispatch(uint32_t commandId, uint32_t sourceId)
  {
    return mCommandSignal.EmitOr(commandId, sourceId);
  }

private:
  CommandSignalType mCommandSignal;
};

class CommandHandler : public ConnectionTracker
{
public:
  void Bind(CommandRouter& router)
  {
    router.CommandSignal().Connect(this, &CommandHandler::OnCommand);
  }

private:
  bool OnCommand(uint32_t commandId, uint32_t)
  {
    return commandId == 1u;
  }
};
```

Keep returning signals narrow and documented in the component that owns them. For most dali-ui view events, application code connects to the signal exposed by the view or trait and follows that signal's callback signature.
